Decision: Write model.py, train.py, predict.py; train for 2 epochs; test predict.py locally; then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous three iterations failed immediately after model loading (~24s) with DDP exit code 1 and no visible traceback. The workspace shows train.py was truncated mid-if-statement, but even if rewritten, a deeper runtime bug exists: UPerHead outputs 1/4-resolution feature maps (128x128 for 512x512 input), while the training loop passed full-resolution masks (512x512) directly to F.cross_entropy, which causes an immediate shape-mismatch RuntimeError on the very first training step. This explains the silent crash (torchrun swallows the traceback). Analysis: I must fix the spatial resolution mismatch by downsampling ground-truth masks to the logits resolution before loss computation, and upsample low-res predictions back to mask resolution during validation mIoU calculation. I also need to ensure the files are syntactically complete. Decision: Rewrite model.py, train.py, and predict.py. In train.py, downsample masks via nearest-neighbor interpolation to match logits shape. In evaluate, upsample preds via nearest-neighbor to match mask shape. Run a quick shape-check, then launch 2-epoch DDP training with batch_size=4 per GPU (safe for ConvNeXt-Large), test predict.py locally, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4 crashed during training with ValueError from BatchNorm on a [1,512,1,1] tensor inside the PPM adaptive-pool branch. This happens when a DDP rank receives a single sample in the last batch. The train.py file was also truncated mid-file in the workspace display. Analysis: We need a complete, syntactically correct train.py that (1) sets drop_last=True in the training DataLoader to eliminate size-1 batches, (2) downsamples ground-truth masks to match the 1/4-resolution logits before loss, and (3) upsamples predictions during validation mIoU computation. Decision: Rewrite train.py from scratch with these fixes, plus SyncBatchNorm conversion for stability, mixed-precision training, and end-of-epoch validation. Run a 2-epoch DDP training on 4 GPUs to establish a stable baseline checkpoint.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 6 crashed in predict.py with 'ValueError: too many values to unpack (expected 2)' when unpacking orig_size. Analysis: PIL img.size returns (W,H). When passed through PyTorch's default_collate, if all images in a batch share the same (W,H), default_collate turns the list of tuples into a tuple of two tensors (widths, heights). Iterating over that tuple yields a tensor of length batch_size, causing the unpack to fail. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to detect and convert collated orig_sizes back into a list of (w,h) tuples. Then run a local smoke-test on the validation set to verify inference completes, and immediately submit eval to get the baseline score.

Remaining time is ~2.4h (146 min) with 14 iterations left. Analysis: Multi-scale resize TTA still processes images at coarse resolutions (max 3200px) and loses fine details. Sliding-window inference at native resolution with 512×512 crops and 50% overlap is a standard technique that typically yields a large mIoU boost over whole-image resize, especially on ADE20K where objects vary in scale. Expected gain: +0.5–1.5 mIoU. Time cost: ~30–45 min on 4 GPUs with batch-4 windows, well within budget. I will also prepare an improved train.py (poly LR, stronger aug, resume) in case time remains for a follow-up training run after seeing the sliding-window score. Decision: Rewrite predict.py with sliding-window + horizontal-flip TTA and batched window inference. Run a smoke test, then full validation inference, and submit eval.

Iteration 18's batched predict.py crashed with SIGKILL because it accumulated full [150,H,W] logits for all 500 images per GPU in RAM. Analysis: To beat/match 0.5132 within the time budget, I must run a safe multi-scale TTA inference that finishes well under 68 min. The fix is to process images sequentially (one at a time) per GPU worker, accumulating logits for only a single image in CPU RAM before saving its PNG. To recover speed, I batch original+flip together in one forward pass (7 forwards per image instead of 14). Memory is safe because only one image accumulator lives on CPU at a time. 4 GPUs × 500 images × 7 forwards ≈ 3,500 forwards/GPU, which at ~0.15 sec each is ~9 min GPU time + overhead, so total wall-clock ~25–35 min. Decision: Rewrite predict.py with this robust single-image-accumulation, batched-flip approach, using the same 7 scales and max_size=3200 that yielded the best score. Do NOT touch model.py or train.py. Launch inference immediately.

import torch

m = SegmentationModel(num_classes=150, aux=True)

print('Unexpected keys:', len(unexpected))
if missing: print(missing[:5])
if unexpected: print(unexpected[:5])
m.eval()
with torch.no_grad():

print('Forward OK', y.shape)
"
